name: Shelly Manne
abstract: Sheldon "Shelly" Manne (June 11, 1920 – September 26, 1984) was an American jazz drummer. Most frequently associated with West Coast jazz, he was known for his versatility and also played in a number of other styles, including Dixieland, swing, bebop, avant-garde jazz, and later fusion.

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Haynes
about_quotes.evidence: I know one year I did a tour in Japan. This was in the ’60s. This was one of the first groups to go to Japan, as a drum thing anyhow. It was Max, Philly, Shelly [Manne], and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy Haynes — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Bert
about_quotes.evidence: The rhythm was Shelly Manne, Bob Carter on bass and Lou Levy on piano. Bill Harris, me and Milty Gold.
about_quotes.doc_title: Eddie Bert interviewed by Monk Rowe, Danbury, Connecticut, November 20, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: the band itself had as its rhythm section Ray Brown, Shelly Manne – and I can’t for the life of me remember who the piano player was [Michael Lang], but it had all the best players on the West Coast in it. So that was something that we had gotten together
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Woody Herman
about_quotes.evidence: the small group included Shelly Manne, Ralph Burns on piano, Red Mitchell on bass, Conte Candoli, Milt Jackson, and that's it -- oh, yeah, and Dave Barber.

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Jolly
about_quotes.evidence: Now when I came out here to work with Shorty’s band, Shelly Manne was playing with him... I learned a lot by listening to him, and we enjoyed each other
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Jolly interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Helen Humes
about_quotes.evidence: you made that album with Benny Carter, Shelly Manne, and Andre Previn... I certainly did. That was one of the best sessions I ever had.
